sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, specifically feeling like something is stuck in his throat, especially with solid foods.
He denies coughing, vomiting blood, or weight loss.
He has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes and norvasc for high blood pressure.
His hemoglobin A1c was previously 7.4.
He reports taking his medications as directed and checking his blood sugars 80% of the time, with numbers within target range.
He denies any side effects from metformin.
Past medical history: Diabetes, high blood pressure, taking metformin 500mg twice a day and norvasc.
He reports improved diet and exercise.
Review of systems: Denies coughing, vomiting blood, weight loss, chest pain, or shortness of breath.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes, norvasc for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital signs: BP 120/50, no other vital signs mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, no swelling or masses.
Thyroid feels normal, no lymph nodes.
Throat exam is clear, no masses or swelling.
Lung exam is clear, heart exam is normal, no murmurs.
Belly exam reveals epigastric tenderness, but no masses or significant swelling.
Back exam is normal, extremity exam is fine, neurological exam is normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with difficulty swallowing, likely due to a narrowing of the throat, possibly a stricture in the esophagus.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing (active); Diabetes (active); High blood pressure (active).
Plan:
I will refer the patient to gastroenterology for a scope to evaluate the esophagus.
I will start the patient on prilosec 20mg once a day for reflux.
I will recommend chewing food frequently and thoroughly, especially hard foods like steak or meat.
I will order another hemoglobin A1c, kidney function tests, and refer the patient to ophthalmology for diabetic retinopathy evaluation.
I will continue the patient on metformin and norvasc as before.
I will have the patient return in a month for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will return in one month for follow-up and to discuss the results of the blood tests and any necessary adjustments to treatment.
